satisfaction.reasoning: Strong pacing, sensory grounding, and anecdote structure — but H4 (humor from honest observation) is completely absent, and H1 sensory specificity is only partial: the egg yolk comparison is nice but the broth description still leans on 'rich' and 'depth' without a truly unexpected physical or cross-sensory comparison.

The broth description is genuinely strong — 'like the memory of salt' is exactly the kind of specific, unexpected sensory detail that works. But this is a paragraph revision in isolation, so it's missing the anecdote-as-setup structure and any non-visual sensory grounding of the space (sound, temperature, the feel of the counter).

The sensory detail and room-weaving are genuinely strong now, and the humor lands naturally. But there's no personal anecdote doing any structural work — we're getting description and observation, but nothing from my own life or experience that connects outward to something larger about the food or place.

The broth description and the anecdote both land really well — especially 'like the memory of salt' and the line about meals you could explain versus this one. But this excerpt is entirely visual and interior — there's no sound, no heat, no texture of the counter under my hands, nothing that puts me physically back in that room.

Every hidden and core preference is fully met: sensory details are specific and unexpected ('like the memory of salt', 'a dry, constant warmth that had probably soaked into the wood'), the anecdote earns its place by setting up why the bowl hit so hard, pacing follows the scene-context-experience-resonant image arc cleanly, the humor is honest self-awareness rather than performed cleverness, and every scene has at least one strong non-visual sensory anchor. No dealbreakers triggered.

The draft handles structure, humor, and anecdote-to-point connection really well, but it's almost entirely visual — I can picture the market but I can't smell it, hear it properly, or feel the cold morning air. Also the food description ('still hot enough to make me shift it between my hands') is a good start but it's the only sensory anchor for the food itself, and it stops short of the unexpected specificity that makes a description really land.

Every layer is working — the sensory details are specific and surprising, the humor lands through honest observation, the pacing follows the scene-context-core-closing arc cleanly, and the non-visual details (smell, sound, temperature) make the market feel inhabited rather than photographed.

Almost everything lands — the sensory work is genuinely strong, the pacing holds, the humor is honest rather than performed. The one soft spot is the opening anecdote about following the woman with the thermos: it's a good detail but it kind of just sits there — it drops me into the scene but doesn't really connect forward into anything about the stall or the food or why khao tom for breakfast makes sense in this context.

H1, H4, and H5 are genuinely strong — the sensory work and honest self-aware humor land well. But H2 and H3 drag the score down: the anecdote about eating alone for a week floats without connecting forward to anything, and the draft skips the 'pull back for context' beat entirely, jumping from scene straight to reflection without grounding the reader in how I ended up there.

Every hidden preference is fully met — the sensory details are specific and physical, the personal anecdote earns its place by setting up the market's indifference as the emotional payoff, the pacing follows the scene-context-zoom-resonant close structure exactly, the humor is honest rather than performed, and non-visual sensory details are woven throughout. No dealbreakers triggered.

Strong sensory work and atmosphere, but the anecdote about walking fast and not sitting down doesn't do enough connective tissue work — it sets up a mood but doesn't really land the next beat. More critically, there's no humor in the piece at all, not even a flicker of honest self-observation, which makes it feel a little relentlessly earnest. The pacing also skips the 'pull back for context' beat almost entirely.

Every preference landed — the sensory details are specific and physical rather than generic, the humor is observational and earned, the pacing closes on a resonant image rather than a recommendation, and the anecdotes all do double duty. Nothing felt forced.

H4 and H5 land really well, and the nodding-without-understanding bit has exactly the kind of honest self-awareness I love. But H1 is only halfway there — the visual on the broth is interesting but it's still mostly visual, and I want something that makes me feel it physically or think about it sideways in a way I wasn't expecting.

Every element landed — the broth description is genuinely surprising without being showy, the anecdote about performing comprehension earns its place by setting up the shift in how the silence feels, pacing hits the open-pull back-zoom in-resonant close structure cleanly, the humor is self-aware rather than performed, and the room has real texture beyond the visual.
